text.title: Consistency and Standards — Framework and Implementation
text.summary: Five consistency types, the prioritization hierarchy, and concrete implementation patterns for visual, functional, and verbal layers.

rule.statement: Apply consistency across all five layers: internal (within product), external (with platform/industry), visual (appearance), functional (behavior), and verbal (language).
rule.rationale: Addressing only visual consistency while ignoring verbal consistency still forces users to relearn terminology between sections — each layer independently increases cognitive load when violated.

rule.statement: When internal and external conventions conflict, always defer to industry conventions first, then platform conventions, then product family conventions.
rule.rationale: Users bring expectations from the wider ecosystem. Violating well-established industry patterns to enforce internal uniqueness always costs more in user confusion than it gains in brand distinctiveness.

rule.statement: Define a single canonical color, size, and spacing value for each semantic role and apply it without exception.
rule.rationale: Even one exception trains designers to treat the system as optional, accelerating drift. The value of a token system is in its 100% adoption.

example.note: Primary action: always #0066CC. Destructive action: always #CC0000. Success: always #00CC66. Warning: always #FFCC00. Spacing base unit: 8px; all margins are multiples (8, 16, 24, 32).

rule.statement: The same gesture or interaction must produce the same result everywhere in the product without exception.
rule.rationale: If swipe-left deletes in one list but navigates in another, users cannot build a reliable mental model and will trigger unintended actions.

example.note: Logo click always goes to home. Back button always goes to previous page. Search always in top area. Single click selects; double click opens/edits.

rule.statement: Choose one term per concept and enforce it across all UI text, documentation, and support content — no synonyms in the same product.
rule.rationale: Using "Create" on one screen and "Add" on another makes users wonder if they are different operations. One term per concept eliminates that doubt.

example.note: Create (not Add/New/Insert). Delete (not Remove/Trash/Erase). Edit (not Modify/Change/Update). Save (not Store/Apply/Confirm). Success (not Done/Complete/OK). Error (not Failed/Problem/Issue).

rule.statement: Measure consistency by counting distinct style variants for each UI element, not by subjective assessment of whether things "look consistent".
rule.rationale: Subjective assessment is not repeatable and cannot track improvement over time. Counting variants (3 button styles found, target 1) produces a trackable metric.
